report:
# Personalized Health Report: Bryant Grizzle

## Introduction

Bryant, we recognize that you've been navigating an incredibly challenging health journey. From your late teens through now at age 28, you've experienced waves of improvement followed by setbacks, tried numerous interventions with temporary relief that fades, and most recently hit an acute crisis with severe nausea, dizziness, and vomiting that has made even basic daily tasks difficult. With your baby due at the end of this month, we understand the urgency of finding stability and real answers.

Your case exemplifies the interconnected nature of bioenergetic health. At the foundation, your body's ability to produce cellular energy has been compromised through multiple pathways: chronic gut dysfunction producing inflammatory endotoxins, liver congestion impairing both detoxification and thyroid hormone activation, depleted hormone production, and a dysregulated stress response. These aren't separate problems—they form a cascade where each dysfunction amplifies the others.

When gut bacteria overgrow and the intestinal barrier becomes permeable, bacterial endotoxins enter circulation and travel to the liver. The liver, already burdened with processing these toxins, becomes inflamed and congested. This hepatic dysfunction then impairs two critical processes: the conversion of inactive thyroid hormone (T4) to active T3, and the synthesis of hormones including testosterone. Without adequate thyroid hormone driving cellular metabolism, and without sufficient androgens supporting stress resilience, the body shifts into a chronic stress state characterized by elevated cortisol, serotonin, and inflammatory signaling. This stress response further damages the gut barrier, worsens bacterial overgrowth, and suppresses both digestive function and liver detoxification—completing a self-reinforcing cycle.

Your recent sharp decline—the severe nausea, dizziness, and weight gain starting in late 2024—likely represents an acute tipping point in this cascade. The 2022 post-infection period when your gallbladder issues began was the initial trigger, but the system has progressively deteriorated. Your previous labs reveal the depth of this dysfunction: severely elevated liver enzymes (ALT 161), critically low testosterone (172 ng/dL), impaired thyroid activation (low T3), and paradoxical B12 elevation suggesting significant bacterial overgrowth. The brown coating on your tongue, loose stools, and severe bloating confirm active gut pathology.

Our approach targets every level of this cascade simultaneously. We will address the microbial overgrowth directly, support gut barrier healing, reduce liver burden, provide the nutrients needed for energy production and hormone synthesis, and optimize thyroid function. This isn't about chasing individual symptoms—it's about restoring the fundamental bioenergetic processes that allow your body to heal itself.

## Assessment Findings

### Previous Diagnostics

Here is your previous information viewed through a new lens:

| Test | Result | Our Range |
|------|--------|-----------|
| **ALT (SGPT)** | Your Result: 161 IU/L (High) | Our range: 0-30 IU/L |

Severely elevated ALT indicates significant liver inflammation and oxidative stress, likely driven by gut-derived endotoxin and impaired detoxification. This liver congestion directly impairs bile production and thyroid hormone conversion (T4→T3), connecting to your gallbladder pain, nausea, and profound fatigue.

---

| Test | Result | Our Range |
|------|--------|-----------|
| **AST (SGOT)** | Your Result: 53 IU/L (High) | Our range: 0-30 IU/L |

Elevated AST confirms liver tissue damage and metabolic stress. The AST elevation alongside very high ALT suggests hepatocellular injury from chronic inflammation, further compromising energy production and bile flow needed for digestion.

---

| Test | Result | Our Range |
|------|--------|-----------|
| **Testosterone, Total** | Your Result: 172 ng/dL (Low) | Our range: >75th percentile (approximately >500 ng/dL for age 28) |

Critically low testosterone at age 28 indicates severe HPA axis suppression and impaired steroidogenesis, likely from chronic inflammation, gut endotoxin, and liver dysfunction. This directly drives your anhedonia, sexual dysfunction, fatigue, and poor stress resilience.

---

| Test | Result | Our Range |
|------|--------|-----------|
| **Sex Hormone Binding Globulin (SHBG)** | Your Result: 10.2 nmol/L (Low) | Our range: 20-40 nmol/L (male) |

Very low SHBG suggests chronically elevated insulin or severe hypothyroidism with poor liver synthesis. Low SHBG increases estrogen activity relative to androgens and indicates metabolic dysfunction, contributing to your hormonal imbalance and inflammation.

---

| Test | Result | Our Range |
|------|--------|-----------|
| **MCV (Mean Corpuscular Volume)** | Your Result: 98 fL (High) | Our range: 85-95 fL |

Elevated MCV suggests either hypothyroidism or functional B-vitamin deficiency despite high serum B12. In context of your low temps (96.5-97.6°F), this points to impaired thyroid function slowing red blood cell maturation and cellular metabolism.

---

| Test | Result | Our Range |
|------|--------|-----------|
| **Vitamin B12** | Your Result: 1718 pg/mL (High) | Our range: 3.4-7 ng/mL for folate; B12 elevated outside typical range |

Extremely elevated B12 likely indicates bacterial overgrowth producing excess B12 or impaired cellular uptake. This paradoxical elevation alongside high MCV suggests functional B12 deficiency at the tissue level, connecting to your severe bloating, gut dysfunction, and neurological symptoms (brain fog).

---

| Test | Result | Our Range |
|------|--------|-----------|
| **LDL Cholesterol** | Your Result: 164 mg/dL (High) | Our range: 70-150 mg/dL |

Elevated LDL with high total cholesterol (224) indicates impaired thyroid hormone activity—thyroid hormones drive cholesterol clearance and conversion to protective steroids. This confirms metabolic slowdown and stress hormone deficiency contributing to your symptoms.

---

| Test | Result | Our Range |
|------|--------|-----------|
| **TSH** | Your Result: 0.940 uIU/mL | Our range: <1.5 uIU/mL |

TSH appears optimal, but in context of low basal temps (96.5-97.6°F), high cholesterol, elevated MCV, and low testosterone, this suggests tissue-level hypothyroidism with poor T4→T3 conversion, likely from liver dysfunction and inflammation suppressing peripheral thyroid activation.

---

| Test | Result | Our Range |
|------|--------|-----------|
| **Free T3** | Your Result: 3.4 pg/mL | Our range: 3.5-4.5 pg/mL |

Free T3 at the low end of range confirms impaired thyroid hormone activation. With elevated liver enzymes and inflammation, the liver cannot efficiently convert T4→T3, resulting in cellular hypothyroidism that manifests as fatigue, cold extremities, digestive dysfunction, and brain fog.

---

| Test | Result | Our Range |
|------|--------|-----------|
| **Ferritin** | Your Result: 273 ng/mL | Our range: 50-150 ng/mL |

Elevated ferritin indicates inflammation and oxidative stress rather than iron overload. This inflammatory marker suggests chronic immune activation, likely from gut permeability and endotoxin, contributing to liver damage and systemic symptoms.

---

| Test | Result | Our Range |
|------|--------|-----------|
| **Glucose (fasting)** | Your Result: 98 mg/dL | Our range: 70-90 mg/dL |

Fasting glucose at upper limit suggests early insulin resistance or stress-driven gluconeogenesis. This mild elevation indicates impaired glucose metabolism and energy production, contributing to your fatigue and anxiety symptoms.

---

| Test | Result | Our Range |
|------|--------|-----------|
| **Cortisol (AM)** | Your Result: 8.6 ug/dL | Our range: 8.6-16.6 mcg/dL (middle 50%) |

Cortisol at the very bottom of the optimal range suggests either appropriate stress response or early HPA axis dysregulation. Given your severe symptoms and low testosterone, this may represent compensated stress with potential for further decline.

---

**Lab Synthesis:** Your labs reveal a unified pattern of impaired energy metabolism: severely elevated liver enzymes indicating hepatic inflammation that blocks thyroid hormone conversion, critically low testosterone from chronic stress and endotoxin exposure, paradoxically high B12 suggesting significant bacterial overgrowth producing excess vitamin while cellular uptake fails, elevated cholesterol and MCV confirming hypothyroid metabolism, and inflammatory ferritin elevation. These findings interconnect through the bioenergetic cascade—gut dysfunction drives liver congestion, which impairs thyroid activation and hormone synthesis, leading to the profound fatigue, cognitive dysfunction, and digestive symptoms you're experiencing.

### Key Patterns

#### Severe Gut Dysfunction and Bacterial Overgrowth

**Data Sources:**
- Questionnaire: Bloating (5/5 severity), distention (5/5), nausea (4/5), belching (4/5), upper abdomen pain (4/5), right upper quadrant pain (4/5), constipation (3/5), diarrhea (3/5)
- Take-home: Bristol stool Type 4-6 (loose/mushy), alternating yellow-brown with floating to dark brown-green, never normal solid brown, vomited from nausea on day 3
- Advisor notes: Dark brown coating on back of tongue, symptoms worsen both immediately after eating and 1-3 hours post-meal
- Labs: Vitamin B12 extremely elevated at 1718 pg/mL (bacterial production), elevated MCV suggesting functional deficiency

**Bioenergetic Implication:** Bacterial and possibly fungal overgrowth in both small and large intestine is producing excessive gas (bloating/distention), inflammatory endotoxins (systemic symptoms), and interfering with nutrient absorption. The bacteria are producing B12 but also generating inflammatory byproducts including D-lactic acid (brain fog) and lipopolysaccharide (LPS) that enters circulation through a compromised gut barrier. This endotoxin burden travels to the liver, triggering the hepatic inflammation seen in your elevated liver enzymes, and systemically activates stress hormones while suppressing testosterone production. The brown tongue coating indicates either reverse peristalsis bringing gut bacteria upward or direct oral colonization from the same organisms overgrown below.

#### Hepatobiliary Congestion and Impaired Detoxification

**Data Sources:**
- Labs: ALT 161 IU/L (normal <30), AST 53 IU/L (normal <30), elevated cholesterol 224 mg/dL, LDL 164 mg/dL
- Questionnaire: Right upper quadrant pain (4/5), upper abdomen pain (4/5), fat intolerance, nausea (4/5), symptoms worsen immediately after eating
- History: Gallbladder issues started after 2022 infection, taking taurine/TUDCA/ox bile/digestive enzymes with only partial relief
- Take-home: Stool alternating colors including dark brown-green (bile issue)

**Bioenergetic Implication:** Your liver is significantly inflamed and congested, struggling under the burden of processing gut-derived endotoxins. This hepatic dysfunction creates a cascade of problems. First, impaired bile production and flow leads to your fat intolerance, right upper quadrant pain, and digestive distress—fats cannot be properly emulsified and digested. Second, the inflamed liver cannot efficiently convert inactive thyroid hormone (T4) to the active form (T3), contributing to tissue hypothyroidism despite normal TSH. Third, liver congestion impairs cholesterol clearance (elevated LDL) and prevents conversion of cholesterol into protective steroid hormones, worsening your low testosterone. The elevated liver enzymes reflect ongoing hepatocellular damage from chronic oxidative stress and inflammation.

#### Tissue Hypothyroidism with Impaired Peripheral Conversion

**Data Sources:**
- Take-home: Basal temperatures 96.53°F, 97.57°F, 96.87°F, 97.59°F (two below optimal 97.8°F threshold)
- Questionnaire: Frequently feel cold especially at extremities (3/5), brain fog and low energy (5/5), afternoon crash (2/5), non-restful sleep (5/5)
- Labs: Free T3 3.4 pg/mL (low end of 3.5-4.5 range), TSH 0.940 (appears normal), elevated MCV 98 fL, elevated cholesterol/LDL
- Take-home: Resting pulse 70-80 (acceptable but could be higher for optimal metabolism)
- History: NDT and T3 supplementation initially helped then caused overstimulation and dizziness at lower doses

**Bioenergetic Implication:** You have classic tissue-level hypothyroidism where peripheral conversion of T4 to T3 is impaired despite normal central thyroid signaling (TSH). Your liver inflammation blocks the conversion process, and chronic stress/inflammation further diverts thyroid metabolism toward inactive reverse T3. The low basal temperatures, cold extremities, elevated cholesterol (thyroid drives cholesterol clearance), and elevated MCV (thyroid regulates RBC maturation) all confirm inadequate thyroid hormone activity at the cellular level. This impaired thyroid function then cascades into poor digestive enzyme secretion (worsening your gut issues), slowed gut motility (contributing to bacterial overgrowth), reduced mitochondrial energy production (severe fatigue), and impaired nervous system function (brain fog, non-restful sleep). Your previous negative response to thyroid supplementation likely occurred because adding thyroid hormone without addressing the underlying liver congestion and gut inflammation simply increased metabolic demand that your system couldn't meet, triggering a stress response.

#### Severe Hormonal Dysregulation and HPA Axis Dysfunction

**Data Sources:**
- Labs: Testosterone 172 ng/dL (critically low for age 28), SHBG 10.2 nmol/L (very low), cortisol 8.6 ug/dL (bottom of optimal range)
- Questionnaire: Loss of pleasure/anhedonia (5/5), lack of sexual interest (5/5), lost morning erections (5/5), irritability (3/5), hyper aware/jittery/restless (4/5), heart pounding/difficulty breathing (4/5), non-restful sleep (5/5), nightmares/sleep paralysis (2/5), wake up frequently (4/5)
- History: Weight gain 35-40 lbs (Nov/Dec 2024 to Mar/Apr 2025) around belly, puffiness/water retention (3/5), progesterone and pregnenolone previously helped initially then stopped working, androsterone caused instability

**Bioenergetic Implication:** Your endocrine system has collapsed under the burden of chronic inflammation and endotoxin exposure. Gut-derived LPS directly suppresses the HPA axis and testicular function, explaining your critically low testosterone and the constellation of symptoms this creates: complete loss of motivation and pleasure (dopamine system depends on androgens), sexual dysfunction, inability to build or maintain muscle mass, impaired stress resilience, and worsening anxiety. The very low SHBG indicates either insulin resistance or severe liver dysfunction, and in this state, the ratio of estrogen to androgen activity increases, promoting inflammation, water retention (puffiness), and abdominal fat deposition. Your cortisol sitting at the bottom of the optimal range suggests your adrenal glands are struggling to maintain even baseline output. The hypervigilance, heart pounding, and sleep disruption indicate your body is relying on adrenaline and noradrenaline to compensate for inadequate cortisol and thyroid hormone, keeping you in a chronic "fight or flight" state that prevents restorative sleep and further depletes your system.

#### Chronic Stress Response and Nervous System Dysregulation

**Data Sources:**
- Questionnaire: Hyper aware/jittery/restless (4/5), heart pounding/difficulty breathing (4/5), irritability (3/5), anxiety progressed to panic feelings over little things, non-restful sleep (5/5), wake up frequently (4/5) for no reason both early and late, trouble falling asleep (2/5), don't dream (3/5), nightmares (2/5)
- History: Anxiety since late teens, worsened significantly during recent decline, job stress, father-in-law death, previous job loss, baby coming soon
- Labs: Cortisol at bottom of range, critically low testosterone, elevated glucose
- Take-home: Somewhat foamy urine (indicates stress hormone/fatty acid excretion)
- Food cravings: Vinegar, salt, carbonated drinks (stress-driven cravings for stimulation and minerals)

**Bioenergetic Implication:** Your nervous system is locked in sympathetic overdrive, compensating for inadequate thyroid and cortisol with elevated adrenaline and noradrenaline. This creates the constant feeling of being "hyper aware" and jittery, the sensation of your heart pounding, and the anxiety that has progressed to panic. The sleep architecture is completely disrupted—you're not reaching deep restorative sleep stages (evidenced by lack of dreams), waking frequently for no apparent reason (nervous system activation), and experiencing nightmares when you do enter REM sleep. This chronic stress state is both a consequence of your metabolic dysfunction (inadequate energy production triggers stress hormones as compensation) and a driver of further dysfunction (stress hormones worsen gut permeability, suppress testosterone, impair thyroid conversion, and damage the liver). The foamy urine indicates your body is excreting stress-related compounds. Your cravings for salt, vinegar, and carbonation all reflect attempts to support adrenal function and acid-base balance under chronic stress.

#### Functional Nutrient Deficiencies Despite Supplementation

**Data Sources:**
- Labs: B12 1718 pg/mL (paradoxically elevated), MCV 98 fL (elevated), ferritin 273 ng/mL (inflammatory elevation)
- History: Benfothiamine, TTFD, thiamine HCL all helped initially then stopped working or became overstimulating, B complex too stimulating, previous zinc supplement caused severe reaction with rash over spleen/pancreas
- Questionnaire: Brain fog (5/5), fatigue (5/5), cold extremities (3/5), skin issues (3/5)
- Take-home: Low-normal pulse (70-80), low basal temps

**Bioenergetic Implication:** You have functional nutrient deficiencies at the tissue level despite supplementation and even elevated serum levels. The paradoxically high B12 alongside elevated MCV indicates bacterial overgrowth is producing B12, but cellular uptake and utilization is impaired—the vitamins aren't making it into mitochondria where they're needed for energy production. Similarly, your elevated ferritin reflects inflammation rather than adequate iron stores, and functional iron deficiency may coexist with the inflammatory elevation. Your severe reactions to previously beneficial supplements (temporary improvement then worsening, or immediate adverse effects) likely reflect: 1) the supplements briefly supporting energy metabolism, increasing metabolic demand, but the underlying dysfunction (gut, liver, thyroid) preventing sustained benefit, 2) detoxification reactions as your congested system tries to process the nutrients, or 3) increased cellular activity revealing the depth of other deficiencies. The B vitamin sensitivity pattern suggests your cells are starved for cofactors needed to run energy metabolism, but gut malabsorption and liver dysfunction prevent proper utilization.

## Recommendations

### Precision Diagnostics

Understanding the full scope of your condition requires objective information beyond symptoms and standard labs. These targeted tests will identify specific pathogens, toxic burdens, and functional deficiencies that are driving your symptoms, allowing us to refine your treatment approach with precision.

| Diagnostic | Provider | Price |
|------------|----------|-------|
| **Comprehensive Stool Analysis with Parasitology** | Precision Point | $303 |

This test is essential given your history of international travel (South Africa, Mexico) and the complex pattern of severe bloating, nausea, alternating constipation/diarrhea, and brown tongue coating. Parasitic infections can persist for years after initial exposure, continuously triggering inflammation and immune dysfunction. Additionally, this comprehensive analysis identifies bacterial and fungal overgrowths with specificity, moving beyond the trial-and-error approach you've experienced. Given your extreme sensitivity to antimicrobials (oregano and camphosal disrupted sleep, nystatin showed no effect), having clear information about what organisms are present will allow targeted treatment with appropriate agents, minimizing adverse reactions while maximizing effectiveness. The post-infection decline you experienced in 2022 may have allowed opportunistic organisms to establish persistent colonization that standard treatments haven't addressed.

---

| Diagnostic | Provider | Price |
|------------|----------|-------|
| **Mycotoxin Profile** | US BioTek Laboratories | $299 |

Your CIRS diagnosis and history of mold remediation, cholestyramine treatment, and persistent neurological symptoms (brain fog, dizziness, nausea) make ongoing mycotoxin burden a critical consideration. While you moved to a mold-tested rental and completed the CIRS protocol, this urine test measures the specific mycotoxins your body is currently processing and excreting. Mycotoxins cause direct mitochondrial dysfunction, impair detoxification pathways, and trigger systemic inflammation—all of which align with your elevated liver enzymes, energy depletion, and multi-system symptoms. If mycotoxins are still present, they would explain why so many interventions provide only temporary relief: you're trying to heal while continuously exposed to compounds that block cellular energy production. This test will either confirm ongoing burden requiring specific binding and detoxification support, or rule out mycotoxins as a current driver, allowing us to focus resources elsewhere.

---

| Diagnostic | Provider | Price |
|------------|----------|-------|
| **Ferritin, Iron and TIBC Panel** | Ulta Lab Tests | $16.90 |

Your elevated ferritin (273 ng/mL) is almost certainly an inflammatory marker rather than indicating iron overload, but we need to confirm your functional iron status. This panel measures serum iron, total iron binding capacity (TIBC), and transferrin saturation alongside ferritin to differentiate between inflammatory ferritin elevation and true iron dysregulation. Iron is essential for energy metabolism—it's required for hemoglobin oxygen transport, mitochondrial electron transport chain function, and numerous enzymatic reactions. However, excess free iron drives oxidative stress and fuels pathogenic bacterial growth. Given your severe fatigue, brain fog, and chronic inflammation, understanding whether you have functional iron deficiency (despite high ferritin), appropriate iron stores, or problematic iron excess will guide whether iron supplementation, iron restriction, or iron-binding strategies are appropriate. This inexpensive test provides critical functional information.

### Diet & Lifestyle Recommendations

#### Foundational Principles

Your nervous system and metabolic function are under extreme stress. Beyond addressing gut dysfunction directly, foundational lifestyle interventions will reduce systemic stress, support circadian rhythm restoration, and create the physiological conditions for healing.

**Sleep & Wake Consistency:** Establish consistent wake and sleep times, prioritizing this even above total sleep duration in the short term. Your circadian rhythm is severely disrupted (waking frequently, lack of dreams, non-restful sleep), and re-entraining this rhythm is foundational for normalizing cortisol patterns, improving gut motility, and supporting thyroid function. Set an alarm for the same wake time every morning regardless of when you fell asleep or how well you slept. Over time, your sleep onset will consolidate. With your baby due soon, establish this pattern now—it will help you handle sleep deprivation better when the baby arrives.

**Sunlight Exposure:** Get morning sunlight within 30-60 minutes of waking, even if just 10-15 minutes. Morning light exposure is the most powerful signal for circadian rhythm entrainment, and it directly lowers cortisol throughout the day while supporting thyroid hormone secretion. Your desk job limits daytime light exposure, so use lunch breaks for outdoor time when possible. Afternoon sunlight exposure (3-5 PM) also supports evening melatonin production. Given your skin issues, do not burn—go inside or into shade if you feel any discomfort. Skip sunscreen when safe (short exposures) as it blocks the UV needed for vitamin D synthesis and the infrared/red light that supports mitochondrial function.

**Eliminate Blue Light at Night:** Your sleep disruption and hypervigilant state require aggressive management of evening light exposure. After sunset, eliminate all blue light—this means device screens, LED lights, and bright overhead lighting. Blue light at night suppresses melatonin and activates stress signaling in the brain, preventing the parasympathetic shift needed for restorative sleep. Use dim incandescent bulbs, salt lamps, or candlelight in the evening. If you must use devices, get quality blue-blocking glasses that filter wavelengths below 550nm. This intervention alone can significantly improve your ability to fall asleep and stay asleep.

**Grounding:** When weather and time permit, place your bare feet on grass or soil for 20-30 minutes. Grounding transfers electrons from the earth to your body, providing antioxidant effects that reduce inflammation, lower cortisol, and improve autonomic nervous system balance. Given your severe sympathetic overdrive (jittery, heart pounding, hypervigilant), grounding can help shift you toward parasympathetic "rest and digest" tone. This is free, side-effect free, and the benefits begin within 30-40 minutes of contact.

**Stress Reduction Priority:** With your baby arriving soon and work stress ongoing, we recognize you cannot eliminate external stressors. However, your body is interpreting everything as a threat because you lack the metabolic resources (thyroid, testosterone, cortisol) for stress resilience. The interventions in this report—addressing gut dysfunction, supporting liver detoxification, providing nutritional cofactors, considering thyroid optimization—will rebuild your physiological stress buffer. In the meantime, any practices that activate your parasympathetic nervous system will help: deep breathing (especially extending exhales), brief meditation, warm baths, gentle movement, or time in nature. Your nervous system is exhausted from chronic sympathetic activation; these practices provide immediate relief even when circumstances don't change.

#### Simplified Nutrition During Acute Phase

Your current state of severe nausea, vomiting, and food intolerance requires a temporarily restricted dietary approach. When the gut is this compromised, attempting to eat a diverse diet simply provides more material for bacterial fermentation and increases the inflammatory burden. We need to calm the system before expanding.

**The Acute Protocol:** For the next 2-4 weeks while initiating antimicrobial treatment, restrict your diet to 5-6 foods that you tolerate best. Based on your description, consider: lean beef or lean shrimp (easily digestible protein), goat milk or goat yogurt (you noted better tolerance than cow dairy), white rice cooked very soft with salt and a small amount of butter or coconut oil (easily digestible carbohydrate), grape juice or orange juice (simple sugars for energy without fiber to ferment), and perhaps eggs if tolerated. The goal is adequate caloric intake (aim for at least 2000 calories to prevent further metabolic suppression) from minimally irritating sources.

Avoid during this acute phase: all vegetables, fibrous foods, beans/legumes, fatty cuts of meat or large amounts of added fats (given your gallbladder pain), high-FODMAP foods, cow's dairy (you identified intolerance), high fructose corn syrup (you identified as trigger), and any foods that have previously caused immediate symptoms. Also continue avoiding polyunsaturated fat sources (seed oils, nuts, fatty fish).

**Implementation:** Eat smaller, more frequent meals rather than large portions that trigger your nausea and gallbladder pain. Focus on thorough chewing to reduce digestive burden. Don't force food when severely nauseated, but prioritize liquid calories (juice, goat milk) to maintain intake. Salt foods to taste—adequate sodium supports blood volume, helps with your dizziness, and is needed for stomach acid production.

**Expansion Timeline:** After 2-3 weeks, if your acute symptoms (nausea, vomiting, severe bloating) have improved by at least 50%, begin reintroducing ONE food at a time every 3-4 days. Start with well-cooked potatoes, then perhaps a simple cooked vegetable like zucchini or carrots, then gradually expand proteins (chicken, different fish). Monitor your response to each addition. The goal is to progressively rebuild dietary diversity as your gut heals, but rushing this process will cause setbacks.

**Rationale:** Low-residue, low-fiber diets have demonstrated significant benefit in IBS and even induced remission in inflammatory bowel diseases by reducing bacterial fermentation, minimizing mechanical irritation of the gut lining, and lowering the substrate available for gas production. Your severe bloating and distention indicate excessive gas production from bacterial fermentation. By temporarily limiting fermentable material while addressing the overgrowth with antimicrobials, we create conditions for gut barrier repair. This is a short-term therapeutic intervention, not a permanent approach—long-term restriction would worsen the problem. But in your current acute state with a baby arriving imminently, we need rapid symptom reduction so you can function.

#### Eliminate Polyunsaturated Fats (PUFA)

Continue your existing efforts to avoid polyunsaturated fats, and tighten this elimination further during the healing phase. Primary sources to eliminate: soybean oil, canola oil, corn oil, cottonseed oil, sunflower oil, safflower oil, rice bran oil, grapeseed oil (these are ubiquitous in packaged foods and restaurants). Also minimize: nuts, seeds, fatty pork, fatty chicken with skin, most fish except lean white fish and shellfish.

PUFAs and their oxidative metabolites suppress metabolic rate, promote systemic inflammation, impair thyroid function, and generate oxidative stress that damages the liver—all directly relevant to your case. Choose fats from butter, ghee, coconut oil, beef tallow, and moderate amounts of olive oil. When eating out (which should be minimal during the acute phase), ask about cooking oils and choose simpler preparations.

### Supplement Recommendations

These recommendations are sequenced intentionally. We begin with antimicrobial intervention to reduce bacterial overgrowth, support gut barrier healing, and address nutrient deficiencies. After 2 weeks of the initial phase, we introduce beneficial bacteria to rebuild healthy flora.

| Supplement | Provider |
|------------|----------|
| **Minocycline** | [ReliableRx](https://www.reliablerxpharmacy.com/minoz-100-mg.html) |

**Dosage and Timing:** Start with 25 mg twice daily (this is 1/4 of a 100mg tablet—you can break it by hand, cut with a knife, or bite it in half, then chew and swallow). Take at least one hour before meals. Start with just 2-3 days at this dose to assess tolerance.

**Notes:** Given your extreme sensitivity to antimicrobials (oregano and camphosal severely disrupted your sleep, nystatin showed no effect), we're choosing minocycline strategically. Unlike the natural antimicrobials you've tried, minocycline is bacteriostatic rather than bactericidal—it stops bacterial reproduction without directly killing bacteria, resulting in a gentler effect with less die-off reaction. Critically, minocycline also has potent anti-inflammatory properties that help minimize the systemic inflammation and adverse reactions you experienced with previous treatments. This dual action (reducing bacterial load plus calming inflammation) respects your sensitivity while effectively addressing the overgrowth indicated by your brown tongue coating, severe bloating, and elevated B12.

Because minocycline is bacteriostatic, full effects may take 2-10 days to manifest after starting. If you see little response after one week at 25mg twice daily, increase to 50mg twice daily for up to another week. These short courses (2-3 weeks maximum) can be repeated if symptoms return, but over time doses should decrease as the underlying problem corrects. Take minocycline during the first 2 weeks of your protocol, then reassess. When ordering from ReliableRx, a prescription is not required—simply pay and the product will arrive. Note that delivery may take several weeks, so start implementing other recommendations while waiting.

---

| Supplement | Provider |
|------------|----------|
| **Tributyrin (CoreBiome)** | [Peak Performance](https://www.amazon.com/Peak-Performance-Corebiome-Tributyrin-Postbiotics/dp/B0B194KJ92/) |

**Dosage and Timing:** 2 capsules per day with food.

**Notes:** Tributyrin delivers butyrate, the primary metabolic fuel for the cells lining your colon (colonocytes). This is essential for your case because butyrate directly strengthens the intestinal barrier (addressing your leaky gut), stimulates protective mucus production, and significantly reduces the intestinal permeability that allows bacterial endotoxins into circulation. Those endotoxins are driving your liver inflammation (ALT 161), systemic symptoms, and hormonal suppression. By fueling colonocytes and repairing tight junctions between intestinal cells, tributyrin helps stop the endotoxin leak at its source. Additionally, butyrate has anti-inflammatory effects and helps regulate the alternating constipation and diarrhea you're experiencing. Start this immediately alongside minocycline—while the antibiotic reduces bacterial overgrowth, tributyrin supports barrier repair so the gut can heal.

---

| Supplement | Provider |
|------------|----------|
| **Grass-Fed Lactoferrin** | [LifeGivingStore](https://lifegivingstore.com/store/grass-fed-lactoferrin/) (use code "Optimize" for 10% off) |

**Dosage and Timing:** Start with 2 scoops (120 mg) per day mixed in water or juice. Can gradually increase up to 1 gram daily if no effect is seen at lower dose.

**Notes:** Lactoferrin addresses multiple issues simultaneously in your case. First, it sequesters free iron, making it unavailable to pathogenic bacteria in your gut—this supports your antimicrobial efforts by starving pathogens of iron they need for growth. Second, it helps modulate your elevated ferritin (273 ng/mL) by reducing iron's inflammatory potential and improving iron regulation. Third, lactoferrin has direct antimicrobial properties and potent immune-regulatory effects that reduce systemic inflammation. This is particularly important given your chronically activated immune system and elevated inflammatory markers. Fourth, lactoferrin supports gut barrier integrity, complementing the tributyrin. Start lactoferrin at the same time as minocycline and tributyrin—the three work synergistically to reduce overgrowth, starve pathogens, calm inflammation, and repair the barrier.

---

| Supplement | Provider |
|------------|----------|
| **L-Glutamine** | [Nutricost](https://www.amazon.com/Nutricost-L-Glutamine-Powder-500G-Glutamine/dp/B00SHXE7VK) |

**Dosage and Timing:** 5 grams (approximately 1 scoop) dissolved in water or juice, three times daily, for at least 8 weeks.

**Notes:** L-glutamine is the primary metabolic fuel for the cells lining your small intestine (enterocytes). While tributyrin fuels colonocytes, glutamine fuels the upper GI tract where much of your pain, bloating, and malabsorption is occurring. Your severe bloating, distention, and the immediate worsening of symptoms after eating indicate significant small intestinal dysfunction. Glutamine directly supports repair of the intestinal lining, reduces permeability (leaky gut), and provides the energy these rapidly-dividing cells need to regenerate. By strengthening the gut barrier, glutamine reduces the passage of food particles, bacterial products, and inflammatory triggers into your bloodstream—this decreases the burden on your already-congested liver. Take glutamine consistently for at least 8 weeks; gut lining repair is a gradual process requiring sustained support. Dissolve in your juice or goat milk for easy consumption.

---

| Supplement | Provider |
|------------|----------|
| **Lactobacillus plantarum** | [SuperSmart](https://us.supersmart.com/article.pl?id=0815&fromid=GGNEW) |

**Dosage and Timing:** 1 capsule per day. START THIS AFTER 2 WEEKS of the antimicrobial phase (minocycline, lactoferrin).

**Notes:** After reducing pathogenic bacterial overgrowth with minocycline, you need to rebuild healthy gut flora. We're using L. plantarum specifically because this strain has unique histamine-degrading properties. Histamine elevation (from mast cell activation, gut inflammation, or bacterial production of histamine) contributes to anxiety, gut hypersensitivity, nausea, and many of your symptoms. L. plantarum helps restore microbial balance while actively degrading histamine, reducing your gut's inflammatory signaling. This is a targeted, single-strain approach rather than a broad probiotic blend, which is appropriate given your sensitivity to interventions. Do not start this probiotic until you've completed at least 2 weeks of antimicrobial treatment—introducing beneficial bacteria while pathogenic overgrowth is still dominant often fails or worsens symptoms. Once you've cleared space, L. plantarum can establish and provide ongoing benefit.

Vitamin K2 reduces pathogenic bacteria and inflammation in the intestines while improving gut structural integrity. The synergistic combination supports steroidogenesis (hormone production), immune regulation, and gut healing. This explains your severe brain fog, fatigue, and why previous B vitamin supplementation (benfothiamine, TTFD, B complex) initially helped then stopped working or became "too stimulating." Your cells are starved for B vitamin cofactors, but when you provide them, the brief increase in metabolic activity reveals the depth of other limitations (thyroid, mitochondrial dysfunction) and triggers a stress response.

This liquid B complex allows extremely precise low-dose titration. Start with just 6 drops—far below typical doses—to support energy metabolism gently without overwhelming your system. B vitamins are essential cofactors for converting food into cellular energy (ATP), supporting neurotransmitter production (explaining brain fog), and maintaining nervous system function. The liquid format and ability to start at minimal doses respects your sensitivity while providing the cofactors you desperately need. Take in the morning as B vitamins can be activating. Build up slowly, allowing your system to adapt. These vitamins work best alongside thyroid optimization and gut healing, not in isolation.

## Conclusion

Bryant, your case demonstrates how a single initial trigger—likely the 2022 infection combined with your earlier travel exposures—can cascade through interconnected systems to create widespread dysfunction. The post-infection gut damage allowed bacterial overgrowth to establish, producing endotoxins that inflamed your liver. Liver inflammation then blocked thyroid hormone conversion and impaired detoxification, leading to cellular hypothyroidism and toxic accumulation. The chronic endotoxin exposure and inflammation suppressed your testosterone production, removing a primary buffer against stress and creating the hormonal collapse manifesting as anhedonia, sexual dysfunction, and severe anxiety. Without adequate thyroid and testosterone, your nervous system relied on adrenaline to compensate, locking you in sympathetic overdrive that prevents restorative sleep and further damages the gut. Each dysfunction amplifies the others.

The comprehensive approach outlined here interrupts this cascade at multiple points simultaneously. We address the microbial overgrowth directly with minocycline while supporting your sensitivity with its anti-inflammatory properties. We repair the gut barrier with tributyrin and glutamine to stop endotoxin leakage. We support liver function by reducing the toxic burden while providing the nutrients needed for detoxification and thyroid conversion. We deliver fat-soluble vitamins topically to support hormone synthesis, and we carefully replenish depleted B vitamins and minerals with forms that respect your reactivity. The diagnostic testing will identify any persistent parasitic infections or mycotoxin burden that requires specific treatment.

This is not a quick fix. Your system has been compromised for years, with acute worsening in recent months. Realistic expectations are essential: you should see some reduction in the most acute symptoms (nausea, vomiting, severe bloating) within 2-3 weeks as the antimicrobial phase takes effect and the gut begins healing. Energy improvements and cognitive clearing typically take 4-8 weeks as nutrient repletion occurs and inflammation reduces. Hormonal recovery (testosterone, thyroid optimization, sleep architecture normalization) requires 3-6 months of consistent support. Your sensitivity to interventions means we may need to adjust doses, change timing, or substitute products based on your individual response.

With your baby arriving at the end of this month, we understand the urgency of achieving at least baseline stability. Focus first on tolerating the simplified diet, starting minocycline (order immediately given shipping delays), and beginning tributyrin, lactoferrin, and glutamine for gut support. These core interventions target the most severe acute symptoms. Add the topical vitamins and liquid minerals when they arrive, starting at the conservative doses recommended. Keep detailed notes on your symptoms and any responses to the interventions—this feedback allows us to refine the approach.

**Important Safety Note:** This protocol includes prescription medication (minocycline) obtained without traditional prescription oversight, multiple supplements, and significant dietary changes in a complex case. Stay in close contact with your Prism advisor. Report any severe reactions, worsening symptoms, or concerning changes immediately. The antimicrobial phase can cause temporary worsening (die-off reactions) as bacteria are reduced, but severe or prolonged worsening requires reassessment. Similarly, if you experience adverse reactions to any supplement, discontinue it and consult your advisor before reintroducing.

You've been driving the struggle bus, as you said, for far too long. This comprehensive, systems-level approach gives your body the support it needs to exit the chronic stress state and begin genuine healing. The path forward requires patience, careful implementation, and willingness to adjust based on your responses—but the fundamental bioenergetic principles we're addressing offer a real pathway out of this crisis.
